AMERICAN 
CANYON 
Easterly extension of American Canyon Road to meet a new 
stretch of James Road giving signalized access to McKnight Acres 
and Rancho del Mar will be built this spring. A second major part 
of this project is the construction of a bicycle and pedestrian path 
linking Elliott Drive in Napa Square with James Road in McKnight 
Acres. Construction schedules will depend on winter rains. 
The state highway funding crisis has caught up 
with the signals at Rio del Mar and Highway 29 
which have been planned for since June, 1973 
and included in our County budget since last 
May. We cannot begin construction until the 
State funds are ready and the date for starting 
Napa Phones 
(after 1/19/76) 
American Canyon 
Toll Free 
construction has slipped from September, 1975 
to after January, 1976. 
A new 131 space mobilehome park has been 
approved by Napa County near the Crescent 
Drive-In. No opening date has yet been establish· 
ed. 
The recent election held in the Water District 
concerned the annexation of Rancho del Mar 
to the \iV<lter district. The positive outcome of 
that election will have an impact on American 
Canyon's residents' ability to work together on 
community projects such as recreation.

It is easy to talk about person-to-person government but 
difficult to make it work. This newsletter questionnaire is 
my attempt to expand my lines of communication with 
the residents of the 5th District. 
NAPA RIVER PROJECT 
After more than 15 years of planning, the Napa River 
Flood Control Project was approved by the Board. The 
plans call for a new channel and park through the City of 
Napa and new wildlife and natural areas south of the 
Maxwell Bridge. 
TRANSPORTATION 
Funding problems at the State levcl are delaying the new 
Napa Bypass (HIr\IY. 29) designed to replace the Imola 
bottleneck. The Board hopes, however, that that project 
will be completed as planned since over $10,000,000 are 
currently being spent on the bridge itself. The Lombard 
Overpass at the railroad tracks in American Canyon now 
has No.1 priority in California and will hopefully be under 
construction late next year. Another project affecting the 
5th District is the addition of bike lanes to the Maxwell 
Bridge giving access to Napa Coll<'ge and Napa State 
Hospital to bike riders and pedestrians from River Park, 
Green Meadows, the Gordon Tract, and the Hemlock -
South Coombs area. 
CRIMINAL JUSTICE 
As you can see from the chart at the bottom of this. page 
the Criminal Justice area took the largest share of the pro-
perty tax dollar last year. This year the Board of Super-
visors working with the Judges, Sheriff, District Attorney 
and Chief Probation Officer, are trying to keep expenses 
down by creating new correctional and diversionary pro-
grams to avoid the growing expenses of overloaded courts 
and enforcement agencies. 
PARKS & RECREATION 
The long awaited Napa County Park & Recreation Master 
Plan will soon be ready for public comment. $175,000 of 
revenue sharing funds have already been set aside to help 
build recreational facilities for those communities willing 
to bear the annual operating 
and maintenance costs. 
Public hearings on the Master Plan will be held later this 
year. 
LAND -
USE 
The new land-use element of the Napa County 
.>neral 
Plan makes major changes only in the Coombsvi!le, Jami-
son Canyon, and outlying area of the 5th District. The 
primary impact is to raise the minimum parcel size for 
NEW lot splits from 1 acre to 20 acres in the Coombsville 
area and to 40 acres between Soscol Rodge and the County 
line. These new sizes do NOT affect lots already existin~ 
or any lands in the American Canyon fire and water 
districts. The main result of the new rules will be to guide 
new residential and commercial growth into American 
Canyon and the City of Napa while keeping intact the 
agricultural, industrial and open space resources of the 
outlying land.

Chapter 2 (82041.5) of the Political Reform Act provides this 
definition: 
"Mass mailing" means two hundred or more identical 
or nearly identical pieces of mail, but does not include a form 
letter or other mail which is sent in response to a request, 
letter or other inquiry. 
Chapter 4, Article 3 (84305) provides this prohibition: 
No 
person shall make an expenditure for the purpose of sending a 
mass mailing unless the postage is paid by postage meter or 
the mail is sent by first class or third class bulk rate mail. 
The bulk rate number or meter number shall be stated in a cam-
paign statement, and a copy of every mass mailing in support 
of or in opposition to a state candidate or state measure shall 
be sent to the Commission. 
Such copies sent to the Commission 
shall be public records. 
Question #1: 
Would the above requirements in any way apply to 
an incumbent legislator who may routinely mail to his constit-
uents unsolicited mailings of 200 or more pieces to let selected 
occupational groups know about legislation which may affect 
their profession, e.g., legislation relating to barbers, beau-
ticians, teachers, doctors, etc.? If so, would the requirements 
apply both prior to and after declaration of candidacy has been 
filed?

QUINN, COMMISSIONER, DISSENTING IN PART: 
This dissenting opinion does not question the 
basic soundness nor direction of most of the majorit~ opin-
ion. 
I disagree only with the majority's conclusion 'in part 
(2) that a mass mailing which comes under the requirements 
of the first sentence of Government Code Section 84305 need 
not in every case have a copy sent to the Commission. 
I 
believe that Section 84305 must be taken as a whole. 
It is 
a single paragraph and establishes a single standard. 
Each 
time a mass mailing comes under its requirements a copy must 
be sent to the Commission. 
A mass mailing which is a "reportable expenditure" 
must be mailed according to the specified procedures of 
Section 84305. 
I believe that a copy of any such mailing 
also must be sent to the Commission at the time it is mailed. 
To read the language "in support of or in opposition to a 
state cand 
ate" as setting up a separate standard misinter-
prets Section 84305.

Chapter 2 (82041.5) of the Political Reform Act provides this 
definition: 
"Mass mailing" means two hundred or more identical 
or nearly identical pieces of mail, but does not include a form 
letter or other mail which is sent in response to a request, 
letter or other inquiry. 
Chapter 4, Article 3 (84305) provides this prohibition: 
No 
person shall make an expenditure for the purpose of sending a 
mass mailing unless the postage is paid by postage meter or 
the mail is sent by first class or third class bulk rate mail. 
The bulk rate number or meter number shall be stated in a cam-
paign statement, and a copy of every mass mailing in support 
of or in opposition to a state candidate or state measure shall 
be sent to the Commission. 
Such copies sent to the Commission 
shall be public records.

an incumbent legislator who may routinely mail to his constit-
uents unsolicited mailings of 200 or more pieces to let selected 
occupational groups know about legislation which may affect 
their profession, e.g., legislation relating to barbers, beau-
ticians, teachers, doctors, etc.? If so, would the requirements 
apply both prior to and after declaration of candidacy has been 
filed?
